new_objections.title: Case assignment protocol deflection to 'Supplementary Methods' fails to resolve outcome-dependent circularity
new_objections.critique: Manuscript states cases assigned to 'probable commune of infection' per 'Supplementary Methods' but provides no detail in Methods section. Walk-forward validation requires temporal integrity: outcome assignment must precede prediction. Retrospective spatial imputation based on epidemiological investigation inherently uses case status (the outcome) to determine location (the predictor space). This is fatal circularity.
new_objections.suggested_response: Provide explicit temporal protocol: (1) Were case locations determined *before* model training for each fold? (2) Were assignment rules fixed and blinded to model output? (3) Provide sensitivity analysis restricting to residence-address cases only (no imputation). Without this, all validation metrics are uninterpretable.

new_objections.title: Burn-in lock date 2026-04-04 remains temporally impossible and prespecification claim is demonstrably false
new_objections.critique: Authors claim Addendum v1.1 locked burn-in exclusion on 2026-04-04, *before* walk-forward output. But test folds 2011–2024 produce output dated earlier than lock date by physical impossibility. Filesystem timestamps cannot rescue this: the decision to exclude folds 2011–2014 is outcome-dependent model rescue. EPV ratio calculation (19.4) is post-hoc justification.
new_objections.suggested_response: Retract prespecification claims for burn-in exclusion. Report all 14 folds as primary analysis (already in Supp Table S3). Acknowledge burn-in exclusion as post-hoc sensitivity analysis. Recalculate all CIs and BSS over full 14-fold set. If Tier 1–2 skill holds, no rescue is needed; if not, model fails confirmatory threshold.

new_objections.title: Calibration assessment entirely absent despite 'probabilistic forecast' framing throughout manuscript
new_objections.critique: Manuscript claims 'probabilistic early warning system' (Abstract, Title, Methods 5.5.5) yet provides zero calibration assessment. Log score rewards sharpness + calibration jointly but does not decompose them. Without calibration plots (reliability diagrams) or Hosmer-Lemeshow / calibration slope, readers cannot determine whether model probabilities are trustworthy for operational thresholds.
new_objections.suggested_response: Add calibration plots (predicted vs observed) for each tier, stratified by walk-forward fold. Report calibration intercept and slope with 95% CI. If model is miscalibrated, recalibrate via isotonic regression or Platt scaling and re-report decision curve analysis. Probabilistic claims require demonstrated calibration, not just discrimination.

new_objections.title: E-value 2.07 presented without confounding DAG, quantitative bias analysis, or unmeasured confounder candidate specification
new_objections.critique: Figure 2 annotates E-value = 2.07 for FSI coefficient, implying unmeasured confounder would need RR ≥ 2.07 with both exposure and outcome to explain away effect. But manuscript provides no DAG, no candidate unmeasured confounders, no sensitivity analysis varying confounder strength. E-value alone is insufficient; VanderWeele requires explicit bias modeling and plausible confounder structure.
new_objections.suggested_response: Provide DAG with candidate unmeasured confounders (e.g., rodent density, human behavior, housing quality). Conduct quantitative bias analysis (e.g., array of confounder–exposure and confounder–outcome RRs). Acknowledge E-value 2.07 is weak threshold and does not establish causality. Remove causal language ('trigger,' 'operational lead time') or provide requisite causal inference infrastructure.

new_objections.title: Decision curve threshold range [0.05, 0.20] remains operationally unjustified and arbitrary without cost–benefit elicitation
new_objections.critique: Methods 5.5.8 states DCA conducted over [0, 0.25] with 'operational range [0.05, 0.20] annotated' but provides no justification. Threshold selection requires explicit cost–benefit ratio or stakeholder-elicited harm:benefit trade-off. Claiming 'operational' range without operationalizing harm/benefit is circular. Results 5.6.6 shows net benefit at p_t=0.05 but no sensitivity to cost ratio.
new_objections.suggested_response: Either (1) elicit cost–benefit ratio from MINSAL/SEREMI stakeholders (cost of false alarm vs benefit of true positive) and derive threshold from that ratio, or (2) present DCA across full range [0, 0.25] without privileging [0.05, 0.20], and state threshold selection is deferred to operational stakeholders. Without cost structure, DCA is descriptive visualization, not decision tool.

new_objections.title: Case assignment protocol circular despite 'Supplementary Methods' deflection—Methods §5.5.1 explicitly states cases assigned to 'probable commune of infection' creating outcome-dependent imputation
new_objections.critique: Methods §5.5.1 states cases 'assigned to their probable commune of infection—rather than residence—following the case reconciliation protocol described in Supplementary Methods.' This is outcome-dependent spatial imputation: case status determines case location. Walk-forward validation then predicts cases at locations defined *by* case status. Circularity is fatal regardless of where protocol is documented.
new_objections.suggested_response: Restrict analysis to residence-based geocodes only (loss of n cases acceptable), or conduct dual analysis: (1) residence-based primary, (2) imputed-location sensitivity with explicit statement that validation metrics are biased upward. Current framing is statistically inadmissible.

new_objections.title: Burn-in lock date 2026-04-04 remains temporally impossible—Addendum v1.1 finalized *after* first walk-forward test-fold output (2011) physically generated
new_objections.critique: Methods §5.5.9 states Addendum v1.1 locked 2026-04-04, defining burn-in exclusion of folds 2011–2014. Test-fold output for 2011 was generated *before* this lock date by physical necessity (walk-forward protocol runs sequentially). Burn-in exclusion is post-hoc model rescue rationalized retrospectively. Pre-specification claim is demonstrably false.
new_objections.suggested_response: Report 14-fold metrics as primary (honest), 10-fold as sensitivity, with explicit statement: 'Burn-in exclusion was defined after observing initial fold performance; primary inference rests on all 14 folds.' Current narrative is temporal fraud.

new_objections.title: Calibration assessment entirely absent despite 'probabilistic forecast' framing in Abstract, Methods §5.5.5, Results §5.6.3—log score does not substitute for calibration plots or tests
new_objections.critique: Abstract: 'three-tier early warning system…probabilistic forecast.' Methods §5.5.5: 'predicted probabilities.' Results §5.6.3: 'forecast skill.' Zero calibration assessment: no calibration plot, no Hosmer-Lemeshow, no reliability diagram. Log score rewards sharpness + calibration but does not decompose them. Probabilistic claims require calibration evidence.
new_objections.suggested_response: Add calibration plots (predicted vs observed frequency) stratified by tier and walk-forward fold, with bootstrapped calibration-in-the-large/calibration slope (Van Calster et al. 2016). If poorly calibrated, retract 'probabilistic' framing and reframe as discrimination-only risk ranking.

new_objections.title: E-value 2.07 cited in Figure 2 caption without confounding DAG, unmeasured confounder candidates, or quantitative bias analysis—insufficient for causal inference claims
new_objections.critique: Figure 2 annotates E-value 2.07 for FSI coefficient. E-value measures unmeasured confounding strength needed to nullify association (VanderWeele & Ding 2017). Manuscript provides zero DAG, zero unmeasured confounder candidates (e.g., rodent trapping intensity, C. culeou vs C. quila spatial overlap), zero quantitative bias analysis. E-value alone is ritual without substance.
new_objections.suggested_response: Either (1) construct explicit confounding DAG with candidate unmeasured confounders + quantitative bias analysis (VanderWeele 2019), or (2) remove E-value and reframe as associational model only. Current invocation is performative causal-inference theatre.

new_objections.title: Decision curve threshold range [0.05, 0.20] remains operationally unjustified—Methods §5.5.8 and Results §5.6.6 provide no cost–benefit elicitation or stakeholder input
new_objections.critique: Methods §5.5.8: 'operational range p_t ∈ [0.05, 0.20] annotated.' Results §5.6.6: 'positive net benefit across p_t ∈ [0.05, 0.20].' No justification: no false-positive cost estimate, no false-negative harm quantification, no CONAF/SEREMI stakeholder elicitation. Threshold range is arbitrary and post-hoc selected to show model superiority.
new_objections.suggested_response: Conduct formal cost–benefit elicitation with CONAF/SEREMI (e.g., patrol cost per false alarm vs. averted HCPS hospitalization cost), derive threshold from empirical harm ratio, or present full DCA curve [0, 0.25] without highlighting arbitrary range. Current annotation is results-driven specification.

new_candidates.title: Harnessing big data for climate‑smart livestock systems: A perspective on resilient animal‑source food security
new_candidates.abstract: Climate change is reshaping livestock production through chronic heat stress, hydrologic extremes, feed and water volatility, and shifting burdens of climate-sensitive diseases. At the same time, the sector is known as a source of methane and nitrous oxide, creating a dual imperative for adaptation and mitigation. This review synthesizes evidence on climate risks to animal health, welfare, and productivity and evaluates how big data, spanning the Internet of Things (IoT), remote sensing, cloud computing, and artificial intelligence (AI), can convert heterogeneous streams of environmental and biological data into farm-to-policy decisions timely. Drawing on international case studies, we map a practical “digital stack” for climate-smart livestock systems: (1) continuous sensing of animals an
new_candidates.reason: Big data integration for climate-sensitive disease risk; remote sensing + IoT framework applicable to FSI methodology

new_candidates.title: Agentic Forecasting using Sequential Bayesian Updating of Linguistic Beliefs
new_candidates.abstract: We present BLF (Bayesian Linguistic Forecaster), an agentic system for binary forecasting that achieves state-of-the-art performance on the ForecastBench benchmark. The system is built on three ideas. (1) A linguistic belief state: a semi-structured representation combining numerical probability estimates with natural-language evidence summaries, updated by the LLM at each step of an iterative tool-use loop. This contrasts with the common approach of appending all retrieved evidence to an ever-growing context. (2) Hierarchical multi-trial aggregation: running $K$ independent trials and combining them using logit-space shrinkage with a data-dependent prior. (3) Hierarchical calibration: Platt scaling with a hierarchical prior, which avoids over-shrinking extreme predictions for sources with
new_candidates.reason: Hierarchical calibration via Platt scaling with data-dependent priors; extends our calibration methodology

new_candidates.title: Spatial prediction of animal plague risk in Qinghai Province, China using MaxEnt modeling: implications for targeted control
new_candidates.abstract: Background Plague, as a highly infectious and fatal zoonotic disease, has shown a resurgence in multiple regions worldwide in recent years. Qinghai Province, a significant natural plague focus of the plateau type in China, has experienced periodic outbreaks. However, existing research has primarily focused on the spatial prediction of marmot-suitable habitats, with relatively few studies targeting the spatial prediction of animal plague risk zones. Methods Data on animal plague occurrence sites in Qinghai Province from 1956 to 2022 were collected. Terrain features, bioclimatic variables, land use types, and human disturbance variables related to plague spatial distribution were extracted. A maximum entropy (MaxEnt) model was used to construct a spatial prediction model for animal plague ri
new_candidates.reason: MaxEnt spatial prediction for zoonotic disease; comparable eco-epidemiological modeling framework

new_candidates.title: A Bayesian Hidden Markov Framework for Early Prediction of Crohn’s Disease Flares: Integrating Wearable Sensors with Clinical Biomarkers via Hitting-Time Analysis
new_candidates.abstract: When laboratory biomarkers are collected under symptom-triggered sampling, standard methods assume observation timing is independent of disease state (missing-at-random). This assumption is false in clinical practice, as sicker patients are tested more frequently. Ignoring this dependence provably biases posterior inference. This paper shows that explicitly modeling state-dependent laboratory sampling inside the Bayesian filtering recursion materially alters posterior dynamics and flare-prediction performance. Without this correction, threshold-based or feature-engineered supervised approaches cannot in general recover unbiased state estimates. The framework formalizes flare events as first hitting times in absorbing Markov chains and integrates continuous wearables with intermittent bioma
new_candidates.reason: Bayesian HMM for early prediction with lagged sensors; methodological analogy to lagged FSI GLMM framework

new_candidates.title: A Divergence-Based Method for Weighting and Averaging Model Predictions
new_candidates.abstract: This paper uses a minimum divergence framework to introduce a new way of calculating model weights that can be used to average probabilistic predictions from statistical and machine learning models. The method is general and can be applied regardless of whether the models under consideration are fit to data using frequentist, Bayesian, or some other fitting method. The proposed method is motivated in two different ways and is shown empirically to perform better than or on a par with standard model averaging methods, including model stacking and model averaging that relies on Akaike-style negative exponentiated model weighting, especially when the sample size is small. Our theoretical analysis explains why the method has a small-sample advantage.
new_candidates.reason: Novel divergence-based model weighting framework; direct relevance to ensemble/stacking approaches for prediction

new_candidates.title: A Tale of Two Variances: When Single-Seed Benchmarks Fail in Bayesian Deep Learning
new_candidates.abstract: In limited-data settings, a single endpoint mean of an evaluation metric such as the Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS) is itself a random variable, yet it is routinely reported as if it were a stable property of the method. We study when this practice fails. Using 50 independent repetitions across six regression datasets, we show that CRPS variance trajectories differ substantially across methods and are not always well described by a smooth power-law decay. Methods with a learned heteroscedastic variance head, namely MAP and Deep Ensembles, can develop pronounced, reproducible variance peaks at intermediate training sizes on real datasets, whereas MC Dropout and Bayes by Backprop typically show smooth variance contraction. These peaks have direct practical consequences: at the va
new_candidates.reason: Variance decomposition in probabilistic scoring (CRPS); directly relevant to reliability and calibration assessment

new_candidates.title: Deep Learning for Credit Risk Prediction: A Survey of Methods, Applications, and Challenges
new_candidates.abstract: Credit risk prediction is central to financial stability and regulatory compliance, guiding lending decisions and portfolio risk management. While traditional approaches such as logistic regression and tree-based models have long been the industry standard, recent advances in deep learning (DL) have introduced architectures capable of capturing complex nonlinearities, temporal dynamics, and relational dependencies in borrower data. This study provides a comprehensive review of DL methods applied to credit risk prediction, covering multi-layer perceptron, recurrent and convolutional neural networks, transformer, and graph neural networks. We examine benchmark and large-scale datasets, highlight peer-reviewed applications across corporate, consumer, and peer-to-peer lending, and evaluate the
new_candidates.reason: Comprehensive DL review for risk prediction; methods section for GLMM comparison and temporal model justification

new_candidates.title: Recipes for Calibration Checks in Safety-Critical Applications
new_candidates.abstract: Safety-critical prediction systems, such as autonomous vehicles, weather forecasters, and medical monitors, commonly rely on probabilistic forecasters. These forecasters make predictions about possible future outcomes, and their quality and robustness needs to be validated and certified. Often, only accuracy -- the mean of the predictions -- is evaluated against true outcomes. However, for safety-critical scenarios and decision making under uncertainty, the full distributional properties of the forecasts should be checked: do the observed prediction errors actually follow the forecasted probability distributions? To this end, we introduce a framework for calibration checks: statistical tests that validate distributional properties of forecasts when measured over many samples. In order to s
new_candidates.reason: Framework for calibration checks validates distributional forecasts - directly applicable to our Brier/BSS/RPS methodology

new_candidates.title: Tempered Fractional Gradient Descent for Stacked Ensembles in Smart Grid Stability Prediction: Improving Calibration and Reliability
new_candidates.abstract: This paper introduces a reliability-centric stacking framework for smart-grid stability prediction, considered here as the binary classification of stable versus unstable operating conditions in a benchmark smart-grid dataset. A key methodological strength of the proposed approach is its leakage-safe evaluation protocol, which combines strict outer cross-validation with out-of-fold meta-features so that performance is assessed without reusing validation information. The meta-learner is trained with Tempered Fractional Gradient Descent (TFGD), a history-aware optimizer designed to stabilize meta-level learning and improve the trustworthiness of probability estimates. On the UCI Electrical Grid Stability dataset, the proposed TFGD-stacking framework preserves strong discrimination performanc
new_candidates.reason: Reliability-centric prediction with strict cross-validation protocol - mirrors our leakage-safe evaluation and calibration focus

new_candidates.title: TELEMETRY PRIMACY UNDER REGIME SHIFT: A CONTROLLED BENCHMARK OF COMMUNICATION-DEGRADATION EARLY WARNING FOR AUTONOMOUS AERIAL LINKS
new_candidates.abstract: Autonomous aerial systems rely on stable wireless links for control, telemetry exchange, and mission continuity. In practice, these links can degrade under changing load, interference, and difficult operating conditions, which makes early warning important. This paper studies communication-degradation early warning using a controlled 6G telemetry benchmark, while treating UAV-assisted and autonomous aerial links as the motivating application rather than as a direct data source. We formulate the task as next-interval risk prediction from recent telemetry and client-profile information, and evaluate generalization with a leave-one-regime-out protocol across four operating regimes. To test whether added temporal complexity is truly useful, we compare strong aggregate baselines with temporal a
new_candidates.reason: Early warning under regime shift with temporal risk prediction - conceptual parallel to our FSI-lag operational warning system
